clinicalStem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 3-day history of worsening left ear pain, fever of 39.2°C, and now new-onset swelling and erythema behind the left ear. His mother reports he was treated with oral amoxicillin for acute otitis media 5 days ago but did not improve. On examination, the left pinna is displaced anteriorly and inferiorly, the post-auricular sulcus is obliterated, and there is fluctuant swelling over the mastoid process. He appears toxic. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Switch oral antibiotics to co-amoxiclav and review in 48 hours
Admit, commence IV antibiotics, and arrange urgent surgical mastoidectomy with cortical drainage
Perform myringotomy alone under local anaesthesia in the emergency department
Arrange CT scan of the mastoid and defer surgery pending radiology report
Increase dose of current amoxicillin and add oral metronidazole
explanation: This child has acute coalescent mastoiditis with a subperiosteal abscess, evidenced by post-auricular swelling, pinna displacement, and systemic toxicity despite oral antibiotics. This constitutes a surgical emergency requiring admission, high-dose IV antibiotics (e.g. ceftriaxone), and urgent cortical mastoidectomy with drainage. Delayed or conservative management risks intracranial extension (meningitis, sigmoid sinus thrombosis, cerebral abscess).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics are inadequate for coalescent mastoiditis with abscess formation; this child is systemically unwell and has already failed oral therapy.
Correct — this is the definitive management combining source control (surgery) with systemic antimicrobial therapy.
Myringotomy alone does not drain a subperiosteal abscess and is insufficient when the mastoid cortex has coalesced; it may be an adjunct but not the primary intervention here.
CT scan is useful to delineate intracranial extension but must not delay surgical drainage when there is clear clinical evidence of a subperiosteal abscess and systemic toxicity.
Oral antibiotics and dose escalation are wholly inappropriate for a child who is toxic and has failed outpatient therapy; parenteral therapy and surgery are required.
clinicalPearl: Pinna displacement anteroinferiorly with obliteration of the post-auricular sulcus in a child with AOM = subperiosteal abscess from coalescent mastoiditis — surgical emergency.
keyClue: Anterior-inferior pinna displacement, obliterated post-auricular sulcus, and fluctuant mastoid swelling after failed oral antibiotics
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT before acting — imaging should not delay surgical drainage when clinical signs are unambiguous
missedConcept: Coalescent mastoiditis with subperiosteal abscess requires surgical drainage; conservative management is for non-coalescent mastoiditis without abscess
redFlagReason: Untreated coalescent mastoiditis can cause intracranial sepsis (meningitis, cerebral abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis) — delay risks life-threatening complications

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the GP having woken up that morning with complete hearing loss in her right ear and mild tinnitus. She denies recent trauma, ear discharge, or systemic illness. Otoscopy reveals a normal tympanic membrane bilaterally. Tuning fork tests show Weber lateralising to the left and Rinne positive bilaterally. Pure tone audiometry confirms a right-sided sensorineural hearing loss of 60 dB across all frequencies. She is otherwise well. What is the most important immediate management step?
options: Refer routinely to audiology for hearing aid assessment in 6–8 weeks
Start a course of oral corticosteroids (e.g. prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day) and arrange urgent ENT review within 24–48 hours
Request MRI internal auditory meatus as first step before commencing any treatment
Advise reassurance and watchful waiting as most cases resolve spontaneously
Prescribe antihistamines and a low-salt diet for presumed Meniere's disease
explanation: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL) is defined as a loss of ≥30 dB across ≥3 consecutive frequencies within 72 hours. It is a medical emergency because the window for treatment with systemic corticosteroids (the mainstay of therapy) is time-limited to approximately 2–4 weeks, with best outcomes when treatment is started within the first 24–72 hours. Urgent ENT referral and oral prednisolone should be initiated immediately without waiting for MRI results.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Routine audiology referral over weeks is inappropriate — SSNHL has a narrow treatment window and requires same-day or next-day ENT assessment and corticosteroid initiation.
Correct — early high-dose oral corticosteroids are the evidence-based first-line treatment for idiopathic SSNHL and must be started urgently.
MRI IAM is essential to exclude retrocochlear pathology (e.g. acoustic neuroma) but should not delay empirical corticosteroid treatment — both can occur in parallel.
While spontaneous recovery occurs in about one-third of cases, watchful waiting wastes the treatment window; active treatment improves recovery rates significantly.
There are no features of Meniere's disease here (no episodic vertigo, no fluctuating hearing loss, no aural fullness pattern); antihistamines are not appropriate for SSNHL.
clinicalPearl: Sudden SNHL is an ENT emergency — start high-dose oral prednisolone within 24 hours; don't wait for MRI before treating.
keyClue: Woke up with unilateral hearing loss, normal otoscopy, Rinne positive, Weber lateralising away from the affected ear
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRI before treatment — MRI is needed but must not delay corticosteroid initiation
missedConcept: SSNHL is time-sensitive; the treatment window for corticosteroids closes within 2–4 weeks and outcomes worsen significantly with delayed therapy
redFlagReason: SSNHL is a medical emergency — delay beyond the corticosteroid treatment window results in permanent hearing loss

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 2-week history of brief episodes of intense rotatory vertigo lasting less than 30 seconds, triggered exclusively by rolling over in bed and by tilting her head backwards to look up at a shelf. There is no hearing loss, tinnitus, or neurological symptoms. Examination is normal except for the Dix-Hallpike test, which reproduces her vertigo with a geotropic, torsional nystagmus that fatigues after 10 seconds and has a 3-second latency. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Start prochlorperazine 5 mg three times daily for 4 weeks
Arrange urgent MRI brain to exclude posterior fossa pathology
Perform the Epley canalith repositioning manoeuvre and reassure
Prescribe betahistine 16 mg twice daily for presumed Meniere's disease
Refer to neurology for investigation of a central cause of vertigo
explanation: This is classic posterior canal BPPV: brief position-triggered vertigo, positive Dix-Hallpike with geotropic torsional nystagmus with latency and fatigability. The Epley canalith repositioning manoeuvre is the first-line treatment, with remission rates of approximately 80% after a single session. Vestibular suppressants (prochlorperazine, antihistamines) are not recommended as primary treatment for BPPV as they do not address the underlying pathology and may impair central compensation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Prochlorperazine suppresses vestibular function but does not reposition the otoliths; it is not first-line for BPPV and can hinder central vestibular compensation.

MRI is not required for classic BPPV — the diagnosis is clinical; imaging is indicated only if neurological signs, atypical nystagmus (direction-changing, purely vertical), or risk factors for central pathology are present.
Correct — the Epley manoeuvre repositions displaced otolithic debris from the posterior semicircular canal and is the definitive, evidence-based treatment for posterior canal BPPV.
Betahistine is used for Meniere's disease; this presentation lacks the hallmarks of Meniere's (episodic hearing loss, aural fullness, longer-duration vertigo attacks).
The Dix-Hallpike findings (latency, fatigability, geotropic torsional nystagmus) are entirely consistent with a peripheral cause; central BPPV has no latency and does not fatigue.
clinicalPearl: Classic BPPV on Dix-Hallpike = Epley manoeuvre first line; vestibular suppressants are not indicated and may delay recovery.
keyClue: Brief (<30 s) position-triggered vertigo, positive Dix-Hallpike with latency and fatiguing geotropic torsional nystagmus
commonExamTrap: Prescribing prochlorperazine as treatment — it relieves symptoms but does not cure BPPV and delays compensation
missedConcept: BPPV is caused by canalolithiasis in the posterior semicircular canal; repositioning manoeuvres, not suppressants, are curative

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of left-sided painless, foul-smelling ear discharge and progressive hearing loss. He has a long history of chronic otitis media in childhood. Otoscopy reveals a white pearly mass in the postero-superior pars tensa with keratin debris. CT temporal bone confirms a soft-tissue mass in the epitympanum with erosion of the ossicular chain and early erosion of the lateral semicircular canal. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Start prolonged topical antibiotic-steroid drops and review in 3 months
Refer urgently to ENT for surgical excision (tympanomastoid surgery)
Commence oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for 6 weeks and repeat audiometry
Arrange MRI brain to look for intracranial complications before any ear treatment
Advise aural toilet only and reassure that cholesteatoma is self-limiting
explanation: Cholesteatoma is an acquired keratinising squamous epithelium in the middle ear/mastoid that is locally destructive and will not resolve without surgery. The presence of ossicular erosion and early lateral semicircular canal erosion signifies advancing disease with risk of labyrinthitis, facial nerve injury, and intracranial complications. Urgent ENT referral for tympanomastoid surgery (canal wall-up or canal wall-down mastoidectomy) is mandatory; there is no effective non-surgical treatment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Topical antibiotics may temporarily reduce discharge but do not arrest the progressive bony erosion of cholesteatoma — surgery cannot be deferred.
Correct — surgical excision is the only curative treatment; ossicular and semicircular canal erosion make urgent referral imperative to prevent labyrinthitis or facial nerve palsy.
Antibiotics treat superimposed infection only; they have no effect on the keratin matrix and bone-eroding properties of cholesteatoma.
MRI is useful if intracranial complications are suspected clinically, but it should not delay surgical referral — the operative plan is based on CT temporal bone findings already obtained.
Cholesteatoma is an aggressive, locally destructive lesion that invariably progresses without surgery; it is never self-limiting.
clinicalPearl: Any white pearly mass in the pars tensa/epitympanum with foul discharge = cholesteatoma until proven otherwise — refer urgently for surgery.
keyClue: White pearly mass with keratin debris, ossicular erosion on CT, and foul-smelling painless discharge
commonExamTrap: Treating with topical antibiotics and deferring surgery — antibiotics mask the smell but do nothing to halt bony destruction
missedConcept: Cholesteatoma is exclusively managed surgically; conservative treatment allows ongoing bone erosion and risks serious complications

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presents with a 3-week history of severe, deep-seated left ear pain described as agonising and disproportionate to what appears to be otitis externa on otoscopy. He has not responded to two courses of topical antibiotic drops. He now reports left-sided facial weakness. On examination, the ear canal has granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction, the tympanic membrane is intact, and there is an obvious lower motor neuron left facial nerve palsy. HbA1c is 11.2%. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Switch to a different topical antibiotic preparation and optimise diabetes management
Refer urgently to ENT, admit for IV anti-pseudomonal antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam), and optimise glycaemic control
Perform urgent myringotomy to decompress the ear
Start oral ciprofloxacin and arrange CT mastoid in 2 weeks
Refer to neurology for investigation of the new facial nerve palsy
explanation: Malignant (necrotising) otitis externa is an aggressive infection, almost exclusively caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa, occurring in immunocompromised or diabetic patients. The hallmarks are disproportionate pain, granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction, failure of topical treatment, and cranial nerve involvement (facial nerve palsy here = CN VII). It is a surgical and medical emergency requiring hospital admission, IV anti-pseudomonal antibiotics, and urgent ENT assessment for potential surgical debridement. Topical-only treatment is fatal.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Topical antibiotics are wholly inadequate for necrotising otitis externa, which involves skull base osteomyelitis; this approach will allow the infection to progress to intracranial spread.
Correct — IV anti-pseudomonal antibiotics are the cornerstone of treatment; facial nerve palsy indicates advanced disease with skull base involvement requiring urgent inpatient management.
Myringotomy has no role in malignant otitis externa, which is a soft-tissue and bony infection not involving middle ear fluid.

Oral ciprofloxacin has some anti-pseudomonal activity and may play a role in step-down therapy, but IV treatment is essential initially given skull base involvement and cranial nerve palsy; a 2-week delay for imaging is dangerous.
The facial palsy here is caused by the spreading skull base infection, not a primary neurological condition — the aetiology is clear and neurology referral deflects from urgent ENT treatment.
clinicalPearl: Elderly diabetic + disproportionate ear pain + granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction + cranial nerve palsy = necrotising otitis externa — IV anti-pseudomonal antibiotics urgently.
keyClue: Diabetic patient, granulation tissue at bony-cartilaginous junction, facial nerve palsy, failure of topical drops
commonExamTrap: Treating as simple otitis externa with topical drops — the combination of diabetes, disproportionate pain, granulation tissue, and cranial nerve involvement demands escalation
missedConcept: Malignant otitis externa is a life-threatening skull base osteomyelitis caused by Pseudomonas, not a simple external ear infection
redFlagReason: Skull base osteomyelitis with cranial nerve involvement can progress to meningitis, sigmoid sinus thrombosis, and death if not treated with IV anti-pseudomonal antibiotics urgently

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man with hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe vertigo, nausea, and vomiting that began 12 hours ago. He denies hearing loss or tinnitus. Examination reveals horizontal nystagmus beating away from the left ear (right-beating), which increases on right gaze. The nystagmus does not change direction with gaze. Head impulse test is abnormal (catch-up saccade) on turning the head to the left. He is unable to walk without support. What is the single most important differentiating step to exclude a central cause?
options: Arrange urgent CT brain without contrast
Perform HINTS examination (Head Impulse, Nystagmus direction, Test of Skew) and treat as vestibular neuritis if HINTS is peripheral
Administer prochlorperazine IM and observe for response
Order MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging immediately
Request vestibular evoked myogenic potentials (VEMPs)
explanation: The HINTS examination (abnormal Head Impulse test = peripheral; direction-fixed nystagmus = peripheral; absent skew deviation = peripheral) has greater sensitivity for posterior fossa stroke than early MRI-DWI in acute vertigo (sensitivity ~100% vs ~80% for MRI-DWI in first 24–48 h). This patient already has two peripheral features (abnormal head impulse, direction-fixed nystagmus). Completing the HINTS battery with a skew deviation test allows confident bedside differentiation. CT misses over 90% of posterior fossa infarcts and is not adequate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT brain has very poor sensitivity for posterior fossa infarction (misses >90% of ischaemic strokes in this region) and is inadequate as the sole investigation to exclude central vertigo.

Correct — HINTS examination by a trained clinician is more sensitive than early MRI-DWI for excluding posterior circulation stroke in acute continuous vertigo; a peripheral HINTS pattern allows safe diagnosis of vestibular neuritis.
Response to antiemetics does not reliably differentiate peripheral from central vertigo and must never be used as a diagnostic criterion.
MRI-DWI is the gold standard but can miss up to 20% of posterior fossa infarcts in the first 24–48 hours; HINTS examination should be performed first and guides urgency of imaging.
VEMPs assess saccular and utricular function and are not a bedside tool for acute differentiation of peripheral versus central vertigo in the emergency setting.
clinicalPearl: HINTS (abnormal Head Impulse + unidirectional nystagmus + no skew) has higher sensitivity than early MRI-DWI for posterior circulation stroke — master it for acute vertigo.
keyClue: Abnormal head impulse test to the left, direction-fixed right-beating nystagmus, no hearing loss — clinically peripheral, but HINTS must be completed
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT brain to exclude stroke — CT is near-useless for posterior fossa infarcts; HINTS and MRI-DWI are the correct tools
missedConcept: Early MRI-DWI can be falsely negative for posterior fossa stroke; bedside HINTS examination is the most sensitive initial tool and should precede or accompany imaging
redFlagReason: Posterior circulation stroke presenting as vertigo can be missed if CT is relied upon; HINTS examination is required to detect dangerous central causes before labelling as vestibular neuritis

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old boy is referred by his school with concerns about hearing. His mother reports he often does not respond when called, watches TV at high volume, and has had three episodes of acute otitis media over the past year. Otoscopy shows bilateral retracted dull tympanic membranes with reduced mobility on pneumatic otoscopy. Audiometry shows bilateral conductive hearing loss of 30–35 dB. Tympanometry reveals bilateral type B (flat) curves. His speech development is mildly delayed. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Prescribe a 6-week course of oral amoxicillin
Refer immediately for bilateral grommet insertion
Active observation for 3 months with hearing and language monitoring, followed by grommet insertion if no resolution
Start a 3-month course of intranasal corticosteroid spray
Perform adenoidectomy alone as first-line surgical intervention
explanation: Otitis media with effusion (OME/glue ear) frequently resolves spontaneously — approximately 50% clear within 3 months. UK NICE guidance recommends a watchful waiting period of 3 months with active monitoring of hearing and language development before surgical intervention. If bilateral OME with significant hearing loss persists beyond 3 months and causes developmental or educational impact, grommet insertion (bilateral myringotomy with ventilation tubes) is indicated. Antibiotics are not recommended as primary treatment for chronic OME.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Antibiotics have no role in the primary management of OME; they may treat concurrent acute infection but do not resolve chronic effusion.

Immediate grommet insertion without a watchful waiting period is premature — most cases of OME resolve spontaneously within 3 months and surgery carries risks (anaesthesia, tube complications, tympanosclerosis).
Correct — a 3-month observation period is the evidence-based first step, with grommets reserved for persistent bilateral OME causing significant hearing or developmental impairment.
Intranasal corticosteroids may modestly reduce effusion in short-term studies but are not standard first-line management for paediatric OME and do not replace watchful waiting.
Adenoidectomy alone does not effectively ventilate the middle ear; it may be performed alongside grommet insertion in selected older children with adenoidal hypertrophy.
clinicalPearl: Glue ear in a child — 3 months of watchful waiting before surgery; grommets for persistent bilateral OME with hearing/language impact.
keyClue: Bilateral flat tympanograms, bilateral conductive loss, mild speech delay — classic OME presentation
commonExamTrap: Prescribing antibiotics for OME — antibiotics treat acute infection, not chronic effusion
missedConcept: OME has a high rate of spontaneous resolution; immediate surgical intervention bypasses appropriate conservative management

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old retired teacher presents with a 3-year progressive bilateral hearing difficulty. She finds it particularly hard to follow conversations in noisy environments and struggles to hear high-pitched voices. She denies ear pain, discharge, or vertigo. Family members report she asks for repetition frequently. Otoscopy is normal. Pure tone audiometry shows bilateral symmetrical sensorineural hearing loss sloping steeply across high frequencies (4–8 kHz), with preservation of low frequencies. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Prescribe betahistine 16 mg twice daily
Arrange MRI internal auditory meatus to exclude acoustic neuroma
Refer to audiology for bilateral hearing aid fitting and counselling
Start a course of oral prednisolone for presumed autoimmune inner ear disease
Recommend cerumen removal and reassess in 3 months
explanation: This is classic presbycusis (age-related sensorineural hearing loss): progressive, bilateral, symmetrical, high-frequency loss in an elderly patient with a normal otoscopy and no red-flag features. The management is audiological rehabilitation with bilateral hearing aids. Bilateral fitting improves speech discrimination in noise — the primary complaint. MRI is not indicated for bilateral symmetric presbycusis without asymmetry or red flags; prednisolone is for autoimmune SNHL which is typically progressive over weeks with different characteristics.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Betahistine is used for Meniere's disease; it has no role in presbycusis and would not address the hearing loss.

MRI IAM is indicated for unilateral or asymmetric SNHL to exclude an acoustic neuroma; bilateral symmetric high-frequency loss in a 72-year-old is typical presbycusis and does not warrant routine MRI.
Correct — bilateral hearing aids are the mainstay of management for presbycusis, improving quality of life and communication.
Oral prednisolone is used for autoimmune inner ear disease or sudden SNHL; presbycusis is a degenerative process and steroids are not indicated.
Normal otoscopy has already been confirmed; cerumen removal is not relevant here, and the audiogram confirms sensorineural (not conductive) hearing loss.
clinicalPearl: Bilateral symmetrical high-frequency SNHL in an elderly patient = presbycusis; bilateral hearing aids are the treatment, not steroids or MRI.
keyClue: Elderly patient, bilateral symmetric high-frequency SNHL, normal otoscopy, progressive over years
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRI for bilateral symmetric presbycusis — imaging is indicated only for unilateral or asymmetric SNHL
missedConcept: Presbycusis is managed with hearing rehabilitation, not medication; MRI is unnecessary for the classic symmetric bilateral pattern

clinicalStem: A 25-year-old competitive swimmer presents with 4 days of severe right ear pain, itch, and scanty discharge. She reports pain worsening significantly when she chews food or when the tragus is pressed. Otoscopy reveals a markedly oedematous and erythematous external auditory canal with scanty white debris; the tympanic membrane is not visualised due to swelling. Hearing is preserved. She is afebrile with no lymphadenopathy. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 7 days
Careful aural toilet, topical antibiotic-steroid ear drops, and avoidance of water exposure
Systemic antihistamines and topical hydrocortisone only
Immediate referral to ENT for operative canal debridement under general anaesthesia
Insert a wick of ribbon gauze soaked in aluminium acetate and prescribe oral ciprofloxacin
explanation: Acute diffuse otitis externa (swimmer's ear) is a superficial infection of the external auditory canal skin, most commonly caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa or Staphylococcus aureus. First-line management is topical, not systemic: careful aural toilet (microsuction or cotton bud removal of debris) combined with topical antibiotic-steroid drops (e.g. ciprofloxacin-dexamethasone or neomycin-polymyxin-hydrocortisone). Avoidance of water is essential to prevent perpetuating the moist environment that promotes infection. Oral antibiotics are only indicated if there is significant peri-auricular soft-tissue extension, lymphadenopathy, or fever.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics are not first-line for uncomplicated acute otitis externa; topical therapy achieves far higher drug concentrations at the site of infection with minimal systemic side effects.
Correct — topical antibiotic-steroid drops after aural toilet with water avoidance is the evidence-based first-line management for uncomplicated acute otitis externa.
Antihistamines address itch but do not treat the bacterial infection; topical steroids alone without an antibiotic component are insufficient for established bacterial OE.
Operative debridement under GA is not warranted for uncomplicated OE; this level of intervention is reserved for malignant or necrotising otitis externa or cases with severe canal stenosis.

While an ear wick can be useful when the canal is too swollen to accept drops (allowing medication to reach deeper), oral ciprofloxacin is not indicated in uncomplicated OE; the wick should be combined with topical drops, not systemic antibiotics.
clinicalPearl: Acute otitis externa = topical antibiotic-steroid drops + aural toilet + keep ear dry; oral antibiotics only if systemic features or soft-tissue spread.
keyClue: Swimmer with tragal tenderness, oedematous canal, no systemic features
commonExamTrap: Prescribing oral antibiotics for uncomplicated OE — topical therapy is superior and is the standard of care
missedConcept: Systemic antibiotics are reserved for OE with periauricular extension; topical treatment achieves far higher local drug levels

clinicalStem: A 42-year-old woman with known Meniere's disease presents to the emergency department with a severe acute vertiginous attack lasting 4 hours. She has severe nausea, vomiting, and is unable to stand without falling. She also reports a sensation of right-sided aural fullness and fluctuating tinnitus that is worse today. Her blood pressure is 128/82 mmHg. She has no new neurological symptoms. Which combination of management steps is most appropriate for her acute attack?
options: IV prochlorperazine for symptom control, bed rest, reassurance, and follow-up ENT appointment for long-term management
Urgent MRI brain to exclude acoustic neuroma before administering any treatment
Immediate IV corticosteroids and start betahistine 48 mg three times daily
Arrange urgent audiometry during the acute attack to document the hearing level
Administer IV mannitol to reduce endolymphatic pressure and admit to the neurology ward
explanation: Acute Meniere's attacks are managed symptomatically with vestibular suppressants/antiemetics — prochlorperazine (buccastem or IM/IV) is the first-line emergency treatment for acute vertigo with vomiting. Bed rest, reassurance, and hydration are supportive measures. Long-term preventive strategies (betahistine, low-salt diet, diuretics) are initiated or reviewed at follow-up, not during the acute attack. MRI is not urgently required in a patient with established Meniere's disease presenting with a typical attack; IV mannitol has no role in acute Meniere's management.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — IV prochlorperazine controls nausea and acute vestibular symptoms; long-term management (betahistine, dietary modification) is for the inter-attack period.
MRI may be appropriate at some point in Meniere's work-up but is not an emergency investigation during an acute attack in a patient with known established disease; it should not delay symptom relief.

IV corticosteroids may be used for intratympanic injection in refractory Meniere's or for sudden SNHL within an attack, but systemic IV steroids are not the standard acute management of a typical Meniere's episode; betahistine takes weeks to show benefit and is not started at this dose acutely.

Audiometry during the acute attack is not a priority management step — the clinical diagnosis is established; urgent audiometry does not change immediate management and is impractical in a vomiting, vertiginous patient.
IV mannitol has no evidence base in acute Meniere's disease management; it is not recommended in any guideline for this indication.
clinicalPearl: Acute Meniere's attack = IV/IM prochlorperazine + bed rest; betahistine is for long-term prevention, not the acute episode.
keyClue: Known Meniere's disease, 4-hour vertigo with aural fullness and tinnitus, vomiting — typical acute attack
commonExamTrap: Starting betahistine during the acute attack — it is a long-term prophylactic agent with no acute benefit
missedConcept: Vestibular suppressants treat the acute attack; disease-modifying treatments (betahistine, dietary sodium restriction) address long-term control

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old factory worker is referred for audiological assessment following a workplace health screening. He works in a stamping press environment with noise levels of 95 dB(A) for 8 hours a day. He reports occasional high-pitched ringing in both ears after shifts that resolves overnight. He does not wear hearing protection consistently. Audiometry shows a bilateral notch at 4 kHz with recovery at 8 kHz and normal hearing at low frequencies. What is the most appropriate primary prevention strategy, and what does the audiogram pattern indicate?
options: Prescribe magnesium supplementation and retest in 6 months
The 4 kHz notch indicates Meniere's disease; refer to ENT urgently

Mandatory use of adequate hearing protection (≥SNR 25 dB), engineering noise controls, and removal from exposure pending full audiological review — the 4 kHz notch is pathognomonic of noise-induced hearing loss
Reassure the patient that overnight recovery of tinnitus means no permanent damage has yet occurred and review annually
Start betahistine and refer to audiology for hearing aid fitting
explanation: The bilateral 4 kHz audiometric notch with recovery at 8 kHz is the hallmark of noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL). Temporary threshold shift (manifesting as tinnitus that resolves overnight) is an early warning sign of permanent cochlear damage if exposure continues. The Occupational Safety and Health regulations mandate engineering controls, hearing protection (≥25 dB SNR for 95 dB(A) exposure), and regular audiological surveillance. The patient is at risk of permanent NIHL and must be removed from unprotected high-noise exposure pending implementation of controls.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Magnesium supplementation has been studied experimentally for noise-induced temporary threshold shift but is not a recommended clinical intervention; the primary action is hearing protection and noise reduction.

A 4 kHz notch is not a feature of Meniere's disease, which causes low-to-mid frequency loss. The 4 kHz notch is the classic signature of NIHL and requires occupational management, not ENT emergency referral.
Correct — mandatory hearing protection, engineering noise controls, and audiological surveillance are the evidence-based occupational health interventions for NIHL prevention.

The temporary threshold shift (recoverable tinnitus) indicates that cochlear damage is occurring and will become permanent with continued unprotected exposure; reassurance without action is negligent.
Betahistine has no role in NIHL; hearing aids address established hearing loss but do not prevent progression and are not the priority here — preventing further damage is.
clinicalPearl: Bilateral 4 kHz notch on audiometry = noise-induced hearing loss; temporary tinnitus after noise is an early warning — act now before permanent threshold shift occurs.
keyClue: 4 kHz bilateral notch, noise exposure 95 dB(A), transient post-shift tinnitus — classic early NIHL
commonExamTrap: Reassuring the patient because tinnitus resolves — temporary threshold shift is a sentinel sign of ongoing cochlear damage that will become permanent
missedConcept: Temporary threshold shift warns of incipient permanent NIHL; occupational hearing conservation must be implemented immediately

topic: Chronic suppurative otitis media — safe versus unsafe
clinicalStem: A 50-year-old woman presents with a 10-year history of intermittent right ear discharge, which is now continuous, mucopurulent, and malodorous. She reports mild right-sided hearing loss but no pain, vertigo, or facial weakness. Otoscopy reveals a large central perforation with mucosal polyp and copious discharge. There is no evidence of a retraction pocket or keratinous debris. CT temporal bone shows mucosal thickening in the middle ear without bony erosion. What is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Urgent surgical exploration within 48 hours given the risk of cholesteatoma
Regular aural toilet, topical antibiotic drops (e.g. ciprofloxacin), management of any upper respiratory infection, and elective tympanoplasty when ear is dry
Long-term oral antibiotics alone to prevent further episodes
Refer for hearing aid fitting without further investigation or surgery
Prescribe topical aminoglycoside drops and arrange MRI to exclude intracranial complications
explanation: This describes chronic suppurative otitis media (CSOM) of the 'safe' (tubotympanic) type: central perforation, mucosal disease, no cholesteatoma features (no retraction pocket, no keratin, no bony erosion on CT). Management is conservative first: regular aural toilet and microsuction, topical antibiotic drops (fluoroquinolone preferred — ciprofloxacin is safe when the tympanic membrane is perforated and safe for inner ear), treatment of co-existing upper respiratory disease, and water precautions. Elective tympanoplasty is considered when the ear has been dry for at least 3–6 months to repair the perforation and improve hearing.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Urgent exploration within 48 hours is appropriate for cholesteatoma (unsafe CSOM) with complications; this is safe CSOM without bony erosion or cholesteatoma features — elective management is appropriate.
Correct — medical management to render the ear dry followed by elective tympanoplasty is the standard approach for safe CSOM without complication.
Long-term systemic oral antibiotics are not recommended for CSOM and promote resistance; topical therapy is preferred for ear discharge without systemic features.
Hearing aid fitting alone does not address the ongoing infection or perforation; treating the underlying disease and offering surgical repair is more appropriate.

Aminoglycosides are ototoxic when applied to a perforated ear — they should be avoided in CSOM with perforation. Fluoroquinolone drops (ciprofloxacin) are the safe choice. MRI is not indicated without clinical evidence of intracranial complication.
clinicalPearl: Safe CSOM (central perforation, mucosal disease, no cholesteatoma) = topical ciprofloxacin + aural toilet + elective tympanoplasty; AVOID aminoglycosides in perforated ears.
keyClue: Central perforation, no retraction pocket, no bony erosion on CT, mucopurulent discharge — safe CSOM not cholesteatoma
commonExamTrap: Using aminoglycoside ear drops in a perforated ear — these are ototoxic with middle ear access; ciprofloxacin drops are the safe alternative
missedConcept: Safe CSOM is managed medically first then with elective tympanoplasty; aminoglycosides are contraindicated with tympanic membrane perforations
